title: Baseline retest (no implementation change)
motivation:
Used by the orchestrator integration test (--from-hypothesis path).
Measures the unchanged baseline through the full eval pipeline so the
resulting log entry locks in the first fitness number for phase 8.
hypothesis:
No RTL changes — exercises the worktree -> emit_verilog -> formal ->
cosim -> fpga path on the existing rtl/*.sv to confirm the orchestrator
produces a structured log entry.

changes.description:
Sentinel — no real change. skip_implementation:true below bypasses
the implementation agent, so the file path here is metadata only;
it just has to satisfy the schema's rtl/<file> pattern. core.sv is
always present and untouched.

title: Multi-cycle iterative divider to break the 32-bit DIV/REM critical path
motivation:
The baseline ALU implements DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU as combinational
$signed/unsigned `/` and `%` operators inside the EX-stage ALU
(rtl/alu.sv). A 32-bit divide synthesizes to a long restoring or
non-restoring tree of subtract/compare cells; on FPGA fabric (no
hard divider IP on the Tang Nano 20K's GW2AR-LV) this cone is the
classic Fmax killer. Yosys/nextpnr-himbaechel will route it through
general LUT4s and the 9k-LUT design's worst negative slack will
almost certainly land in this cone.

CoreMark's hot loops (matrix multiply-accumulate, list traversal,
state-machine character classifier) emit *zero* DIV/REM
instructions in the inner kernels — the only divides occur in the
one-time CRC seed setup and the iter/sec calculation that runs
after `stop_time`. So multi-cycling the divider trades essentially
zero IPC for whatever Fmax headroom the combinational divider was
costing. On equivalent RV32 cores published Fmax for Gowin GW2A
fabric typically jumps 25–40% when the iterative divider replaces
the `/` operator.

Multiplier is left alone for this hypothesis. The 32x32 multiply on
the Tang Nano 20K maps to chained 18x18 DSP slices, which are
meaningfully shorter combinationally than the divide cone. Touching
one variable at a time keeps the experiment attribution clean — if
Fmax doesn't move, we know the divider wasn't the bottleneck and a
follow-up hypothesis can pipeline the multiplier next.
hypothesis:
Replace the combinational DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU operators in
rtl/alu.sv with a 33-cycle non-restoring iterative divider, gated
by a start/busy/done handshake. The ALU exposes a `div_busy` flag;
the EX stage stalls the pipeline (holds IF/ID, ID/EX, EX/MEM and
drops a bubble into MEM/WB) for the duration of the divide. When
`done` asserts, the EX/MEM register captures the iterative result
and the pipeline resumes.

Implementation outline:
  - New module rtl/divu.sv: a 33-state FSM operating on an unsigned
    33-bit partial-remainder / 32-bit quotient pair. Sign handling
    is done at the ALU boundary (compute sign of inputs, run unsigned
    divide, fix up sign of quotient and remainder per RV32M rules).
    Edge cases (b == 0; INT_MIN / -1) are short-circuited to a
    single-cycle result and don't enter the FSM.
  - rtl/alu.sv switches DIV/REM ops to consume the iterative divider's
    result. MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU stay combinational (separate
    hypothesis if needed).
  - rtl/alu.sv exposes new ports: `start_div`, `div_busy`, `div_done`.
  - rtl/ex_stage.sv: detect div/rem in `in.ctrl.alu_op`, raise
    `start_div` on the first cycle the op enters EX, stall EX/MEM
    register capture and forward `div_busy` to hazard_unit. ALTOPS
    branch in alu.sv stays combinational (riscv-formal speed).
  - rtl/hazard_unit.sv: new `div_busy` input ORed into stall_if /
    stall_id / stall_ex_mem / hold_mem_wb on the same priority as
    dmem_stall (preserves load-use semantics; div_busy is mutually
    exclusive with dmem_stall because a div is in EX, not MEM).
  - test/test_alu.py: extend to drive the new handshake — issue
    DIV/REM, assert `div_busy` rises, wait for `div_done`, check the
    result word matches the existing 30+ vectors. Keep all current
    bit-exact RV32M semantics (div by 0 -> -1, INT_MIN/-1 -> INT_MIN,
    etc.).

RVFI invariants are preserved because the EX/MEM register only
advances when `div_done` is high; rvfi_order increments exactly
once per div retirement; rvfi_trap, rs1/rs2/rd routing all flow
through the same ctrl_t bundle. Formal `unique` and `liveness`
checks pass as long as the divide FSM has a proven termination
bound (33 cycles), which it does.

changes.description:
New module. 33-cycle non-restoring unsigned divider with
start/busy/done handshake. State: 6-bit counter, 33-bit
partial-remainder accumulator, 32-bit quotient shifter,
32-bit divisor latch. On `start`, latches operands and runs
33 iterations of (shift left, trial subtract, set quotient
bit). On the final iteration drives `done` for one cycle with
`quotient` and `remainder` outputs valid.

Split DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU off the combinational `case (op)`.
Instantiate divu and route signed/unsigned operand prep at the
ALU boundary (per RV32M sign-fixup rules). Add ports `start_div`
(input), `div_busy` (output), `div_done` (output). Combinational
ALU result mux selects the divider output for div/rem ops; all
other ops remain combinational and 0-cycle. ALTOPS ifdef branch
stays exactly as today (XOR-of-add/sub formulas, combinational)
so the per-iteration formal gate keeps its 20-step BMC budget.

Detect div/rem in id_ex_w.ctrl.alu_op. On the first cycle one
enters EX, raise alu.start_div for one cycle. Drive a new
output `div_busy` to top level (consumed by hazard_unit).
EX/MEM register captures only when !div_busy || div_done.
Branch / redirect logic unchanged because no div instruction
is ever a branch / jump.

Add input `div_busy`. Update stall logic so:
  stall_if      |= div_busy
  stall_id      |= div_busy
  stall_ex_mem  |= div_busy
  hold_mem_wb   |= div_busy
div_busy is mutually exclusive with dmem_stall (dmem_stall
requires a memory op in EX/MEM; div_busy means a div is in EX).
No change to load-use / redirect priority.

Wire ex_stage's `div_busy` output into hazard_unit. No top-level
RVFI changes (retirement boundary unchanged).

Update existing M-extension vectors to drive the new
start/busy/done handshake. Each DIV/REM test now toggles
`start_div` for one cycle, waits up to 40 cycles for
`div_done`, then checks the result. All bit-exact RV32M
semantics preserved (b==0 sentinels, INT_MIN/-1 overflow).
Add a new test that issues back-to-back divides to verify the
FSM resets cleanly between ops.

implementation_notes:
# hyp-20260427-002 — multi-cycle iterative divider

## What changed

- **`rtl/divu.sv` (new)** — 33-cycle restoring binary divider FSM. State
  machine: `IDLE → BUSY (32 iters) → DONE (1 cycle) → IDLE`. Operands
  are latched on the IDLE→BUSY transition, so the caller need only
  hold `dividend`/`divisor` valid on the cycle `start` is asserted.
  `quot_q` and `rem_q` retain their values across `IDLE`/`DONE`, so a
  consumer that sees `done` may capture the result on any subsequent
  cycle until the next divide overwrites the operand latches.

- **`rtl/alu.sv`** — added `clock`, `reset`, `start_div`, `div_busy`,
  `div_done` ports. Under non-ALTOPS, instantiates `divu` and applies
  RV32M sign correction at the boundary (absolute value pre-divide,
  two's-complement post-divide). Edge cases (`b == 0`; signed
  `INT_MIN / -1`) are detected combinationally, bypass the FSM, and
  pulse `div_done` in the same cycle `start_div` is asserted with
  `div_busy` held at 0 — so EX/MEM captures the result without a
  stall. Under ALTOPS, the FSM is not instantiated and `div_done` is
  hardwired to 1 (combinational behavior matches the prior ALU; the
  XOR-of-add/sub formulas still drive `out` via the existing case arms).

- **`rtl/ex_stage.sv`** — drives the divider handshake. Two sticky
  flops (`div_started_q`, `div_completed_q`) break the
  `start_div ↔ alu.div_busy` combinational loop and remember a `done`
  observed during a concurrent dmem stall, so the divide is never
  re-triggered. Adds `div_busy` output to top-level. EX/MEM register
  capture is gated on `!stall && !div_pipeline_stall`.

- **`rtl/hazard_unit.sv`** — added `div_busy` input; ORed into
  `stall_if`, `stall_id`, `stall_ex_mem`, `hold_mem_wb`. `flush_id` is
  also gated `&& !div_busy` so the in-flight divide is held (not
  flushed) during the multi-cycle wait.

- **`rtl/core.sv`** — wires `ex_stage.div_busy` to
  `hazard_unit.div_busy` via a new `ex_div_busy` net.

- **`test/test_alu.py`** — extended every DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU vector to
  drive the new handshake (`_check_div`): set operands + pulse
  `start_div`, poll `div_done` (with a 40-cycle timeout), capture
  `out`. Edge cases are exercised on the same code path — they exit
  the polling loop on the first sample because `div_done` is
  combinationally high. Added a new `back_to_back_divides` test that
  issues five consecutive iterative divides to verify the FSM resets
  cleanly between operations. All 30+ existing vectors retained.

- **`test/test_pipeline.py`** — added `divu.sv` to the runner's source
  list (in front of `alu.sv`) so the pipeline DUT compiles. Same
  ordering for the cosim build is automatic since
  `test/cosim/build.sh` globs `rtl/*.sv`.

## Verification

| Gate                                            | Result |
|-------------------------------------------------|--------|
| `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP`     | clean  |
| `verilator --lint-only ... -DRISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS` | clean  |
| `pytest test/` (5 cocotb runners, 21 alu cases) | pass   |
| `make cosim` (selftest.elf + coremark.elf)      | pass   |

CoreMark cosim passing is the strong signal: the full pipeline retires
correctly under RVFI cross-check against the Python ISS for the entire
6 KB performance run, including any DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU emitted by the
CRC seed-setup and iter/sec computation.

## Deviations from the hypothesis plan

1. **`rem_q` is 32 bits, not 33.** The hypothesis described a "33-bit
   partial-remainder accumulator". Inside the FSM the partial
   remainder needs 33 bits during the shift-and-trial-subtract, but
   between iterations the remainder is strictly less than the divisor
   (which is < 2³²), so 32 register bits suffice. The 33rd bit is
   reconstructed combinationally each cycle. This was prompted by a
   Verilator UNUSEDSIGNAL warning on `rem_q[32]` and is logically
   equivalent.

2. **Two trackers in ex_stage, not one.** The hypothesis sketch
   implies a single "div in flight" flag; I needed two:
   `div_started_q` (so the start pulse self-extinguishes after one
   cycle) and `div_completed_q` (so a `div_done` observed during a
   dmem stall isn't lost, and the divide isn't re-triggered when the
   FSM transitions back to IDLE during the stall hold). Without
   `div_completed_q`, a dmem stall coinciding with the divide's
   `done` cycle would either drop the result or cause a re-trigger
   when divu wraps back through IDLE.

3. **Edge cases bypass the FSM.** The hypothesis allowed this
   (`Edge cases (b == 0; INT_MIN / -1) are short-circuited to a
   single-cycle result and don't enter the FSM`); my implementation
   does it by gating `divu_start` to 0 for edge cases and driving
   `alu.div_done = start_div && is_div_op` so EX/MEM captures the
   `edge_result` on the same cycle the divide enters EX.

4. **ALTOPS build hardwires `div_done = 1`.** Under
   `RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS`, `div_done` is permanently asserted so
   ex_stage's `div_pipeline_stall` reduces to 0 — the formal gate
   sees the prior ALU's combinational latency for div/rem, keeping
   the per-iteration BMC depth budget intact. `div_busy` is 0 in
   this build.

## Concerns / things to watch

- **Mutual exclusion claim.** The hypothesis asserts `div_busy` and
  `dmem_stall` are mutually exclusive ("a div is in EX, not MEM").
  Strictly speaking they can coexist: a divide in EX while a
  preceding LOAD/STORE waits on the bus in EX/MEM. The
  implementation handles concurrent assertion correctly — `div_busy`
  and `dmem_stall` both gate `ex_capture` (logical OR), and
  `div_completed_q` survives the stall — but the comment in
  `hazard_unit.sv` is honest about the OR semantics rather than
  promising mutual exclusion.

- **Multiplier untouched.** The hypothesis explicitly chooses to
  leave MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU combinational. If the divider was *not*
  the Fmax bottleneck, the next hypothesis can pipeline the
  multiplier; this one trades zero CoreMark IPC (no inner-loop
  divides) for whatever divider headroom the iterative FSM unlocks.

- **`make formal-deep` not run here.** The fast formal build uses
  `RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS`, which substitutes div/rem with XOR formulas
  — so the per-iteration formal gate proves bypassing/forwarding/trap
  discipline around div/rem, but does NOT prove the iterative
  divider's bit-exact RV32M arithmetic. That's covered by the cocotb
  vectors in `test_alu.py` and by cosim against
  `test/cosim/reference.py` (CoreMark exercises real divides during
  the CRC seed-setup phase). `make formal-deep` would prove
  arithmetic via SMT; it is run periodically per CLAUDE.md, not in
  this hypothesis.
